concepts.definition: conducting the necessary research and investigation to make informed decisions that minimize risk
someone who identifies an idea that no one else has acted on or identified; small business ownership and franchising are also entrepreneurial options
any business, organization, project, or operation of interest that includes a level of risk in acting on an opportunity that has not previously been established
business whose founders’ primary focus is the lifestyle they will attain through becoming entrepreneurs, rather than a primary interest in financial rewards
point at which the venture is sold, at which time the investors receive a return on their investment
method for identifying a problem and determining how it could be translated into an opportunity to create a new venture
entrepreneur who becomes involved in starting multiple entrepreneurial ventures
entrepreneur who has an interest in solving a social, environmental, or economic problem
someone who takes on the daily management as the owner of the venture, accepting, and perhaps enjoying, the daily risks and rewards of managing and building the venture
process of generating as many ideas as possible, even when those ideas do not seem logical; the list of ideas can be conflicting, diverse, and disparate
idea of what an entrepreneur wants a venture to become, what it will look like, what the driving forces are, and what values and culture should surround it

statement of an organization’s reason for being that defines its primary long-term goal, explains what the venture will do, what value it will provide to the end customer, and how it will accomplish that action
method to determine if a goal has been met or not
well-structured and defined goals that are specific, measurable, achievable, relevant, and timely
summary describing the benefits (value) customers can expect from a particular product or service
picture of what the venture will become in the future or what the venture will grow into
comparing one’s own company’s performance with an industry average, a leader within the industry, or a market segment
funding strategy that seeks to optimize use of personal funds and other creative strategies (such as bartering) to minimize cash outflows
level of operations that results in exactly enough revenue to cover costs
management of cash inflows and outflows to support cash needs of the venture
your exploration to discover if entrepreneurship is right for you

(also, freelancer) people or businesses that provide work similar to an employee without being part of the payroll for the contracting business, and who pay their own taxes and pay for their own benefits
significant decision point or significant accomplishment
specific group of consumers for which a company seeks to provide a good or service
startup company or organization that conducts business or is created to satisfy a need

plan for how venture will be funded; how the venture creates value for its stakeholders, including customers; how the venture’s offerings are made and distributed to the end users; and the how income will be generated through this process
system in which individuals, people, and companies have the freedom to make decisions and own property as well as benefit from their own efforts, with government playing a secondary role in oversight
money collected by the business through revenue and funding versus money disbursed by the business through expenses
development of new ideas, opportunities, or ventures through formal research and development processes that focused on the corporation’s strategy and goals

theory developed by Joseph Schumpeter stating that entrepreneurial innovation is the disruptive force that creates and sustains economic growth, though in the process, it destroys established companies and disrupts employment
electronic transactions, particularly over the Internet, for the exchange of goods and services
set of decisions or actions that can be outlined and followed as a guide to developing or adjusting a venture
describes a quality of those individuals who are productive self-starters focused on making changes happen
new idea, process, or product, or a change to an existing product or process
employee who acts as an entrepreneur within an organization, rather than going solo
legal grant of protection for an inventor over the rights, usage, and commercialization of an invention for a set time period
strategy of gaining control over suppliers of raw materials and distributors of finished products to expand or control the relevant supply chain
form of licensing that allows the business (franchisor) to share its business model to expand through various distributors (franchisees) for a fee
one that becomes available, depending on factors such as where you work, your family obligations, your idea or invention, your unique creative expression, or a recent career search or job change
(also, soft open) launching a venture for a limited time frame or audience to gain experience, insights, and feedback about the target market or consumer
organized, step-by-step outline or guide that pulls together the ideas, thoughts, and key steps necessary to help set the stage for entrepreneurial success
structure or outlined process that can be used to accomplish entrepreneurial goals through problem solving, idea generation and validation, and brainstorming
technology that once introduced, displaces established patterns, processes, and systems previously accepted as normal or accepted methods
awareness and focus on identifying an opportunity through solving a problem, and a willingness to move forward to advance that idea
prevent companies from abusing position or power in the market through exclusion or limitation of competitor access to the market

conduct by which companies and their agents abide by the law and respect the rights of their stakeholders, particularly their customers, clients, employees, and the surrounding community and environment
when an individual or organization has interests in multiple areas that come into conflict with each other
grants the creator of a work the exclusive right to reproduce the work, typically for a specified period of time
protects product design
sharing the needed facts and details about a subject in a transparent and truthful way
standards of behavior to which we hold ourselves in our personal and professional lives
impartial, unbiased compliance with rules and standards of what is right, just, and equitable
adherence to a code of moral values implying trustworthiness and incorruptibility because there is unity between what we say and what we do
legally protected result of creative work to turn a unique idea into a product or service

registration that provides the owner the ability to use a name, symbol, jingle, or character in conjunction with a specific product or service, and prevents others from using those same symbols to sell their products
proprietary information, processes, or other internal knowledge that contribute to an organization’s competitive edge in a market
underlying facts, reality, and evidence on a matter
protects product ideas

practice in which a business views itself within a broader context, as a member of society with certain implicit social obligations and responsibility for its own effects on environmental and social well-being
entrepreneurial ventures focused on identifying an environmental problem and creating a product or process to solve that problem
entrepreneurial ventures focused on identifying a societal problem and creating a product or process to solve that problem
practice of preserving resources and operating in a way that is ecologically responsible in the long term
in the ethical context, the process of making ethics a foundation (anchor) of your business so that decision makers act keeping in mind key ethical considerations
inclusion of many types of employees, regardless of differences (race, religion, gender, disability, LGTBQ, socioeconomic, cultural)
way of thinking about or treating employees in a manner that favors or hinders one person or group over another, usually in a way considered to be unfair
human ability to feel what other humans are feeling, which in the context of creativity, innovation, and invention is essential to beginning a process of human-centric design
searching for and finding solutions outside of the organizational structure
generation of ideas in an environment free of judgment or dissension with the goal of creating solutions
development of original ideas

method to focus the design and development decisions of a product on the needs of the customer, typically involving an empathy-driven process to define complex problems and create solutions that address those problems
most critical point of adoption, which often occurs at the end of the early adoption phase, before the early majority steps in and truly confirms (or not) the diffusion of an invention
process that significantly impacts the market by making a product or service more affordable and/or accessible often by smaller companies in an industry
purposeful process of opening up one’s mind to new trains of thought that branch out in many directions from a stated purpose or problem
innovation that modifies an existing product or service
truly novel product, service, or process that, though based on ideas and products that have come before, represents a leap, a creation truly novel and different
process in which inventors and innovators continuously engage with potential customers in order to develop their products and their consumer bases at the same time
free and open thinking in which established patterns of logical thought are purposefully ignored or even challenged
logical, step-by-step process
problem that people have with a product or service that might be addressed by creating a modified version that solves the problem
one based on a new technology, a new advancement in the field, and/or an advancement in a related field that leads to the development of a new product
most often associated with the natural sciences, outlines the process to discover an answer to a question or a problem
early version or prototype of a product that may not be polished or complete but that functions well enough that you can begin to market it or test it with potential users
what the user sees and interacts with
need or desire for the outputs, the product or services

point at which identifiable consumer demand meets the feasibility of satisfying the requested product or service and meets the following conditions: significant market demand, significant market structure and size, significant margins, and resources to support the venture’s success
amount of a product or service produced
statistical factors of a population or group, such as information about age, gender, income, race, or ethnicity
market system in which independent contractors fill a variety of positions
process used to evaluate innovative product ideas, strategies, and marketing trends, focusing on financial resources, skills of the entrepreneurial team, and competition
research that involves gathering new data
research that uses existing data
economy in which some assets are not in use, which may present an entrepreneurial opportunity
method of entrepreneurial problem solving that seeks solutions in ways that are tested and known to be effective
skills entrepreneurs use to pool resources for the purposes of investigating solutions leading to innovative problem solving and competitive advantage
complex analysis of a problem or issue with the goal of solving the problem or making a decision
process of analyzing data and modeling the data into a structure that leads to innovative conclusions
ability to make a quick, effective decision, not letting too much time go by in the process
process of using innovation and creative solutions to close the opportunity gap by resolving complex societal, business, or technological problems

event, usually hosted by a tech company or organization, which brings together programmers and workers with other degrees of specialization within the company, community, or organization to collaborate on a project over a short period of time
method of entrepreneurial problem solving that uses techniques unknown to the market and that bring competitive advantage to an organization
problem solvers who see a problem and ask others for solution ideas
ability to discover clever solutions to obstacles

problem solvers who are autonomous and work on their own, without external influence; they have the ability to see a problem, immediately visualize a possible solution to the problem, and seek to devise a solution
problem solvers who see a problem and begin to consider a path toward solving the problem using a theory
first step of the creative process, which involves recognizing the existence of a gap between the current state and a desired state
teams of amateurs and non-experts working together to form a solution to a problem
stage in which the entrepreneur takes the list of ideas generated and tests each solution for feasibility
stage in which the final solution is assessed
stage of the creative problem-solving process leading to the detailing and formation of idea generation by the entrepreneur
means to enact the plan and test for success
occurs when the entrepreneur notes a gap between societal or customer needs and actual circumstances
process of presenting an idea in a step-by-step graphic format
process of a team creating an unexpected solution for an issue or challenge
focuses on people during design and development
formal presentation in which you ask for something and that is delivered (usually) to potential investors in a startup
brief story of a sentence or two that states a problem affecting many people and offering a solution that is innovative, unique, insightful, or all of these
specific amount of money or type of assistance requested in a pitch
abbreviated pitch, a memorized talk that can get you into the door to put your full pitch deck on display
slide presentation that you create that gives a quick overview of your product and what you’re asking for

formal agreement that the person signing the document will not work for another organization that is a direct competitor of their current employer during employment and usually for a specified length of time after employment ends
contract that carries harsh financial penalties should one party to the agreement disclose what they know of the proprietary information

what a customer sees when they use your product; having a variant of a UI is not necessarily grounds for establishing a patent, but having unique design in the broader sense may help you to secure patent protection
agreement between an individual employee and a company states that ownership of innovations belongs to the company, even if an individual makes substantial contributions to the product
any entrepreneurial competition other than a pitch competition; contests may entail submitting business models, long-form prospectuses, or forms tailored to contest specifications

writing principle useful for preparing contest documents and pitch decks; following this principle, each word or sentence in the submission or presentation should be backed up with a wealth of supporting information and details that go unspoken in the pitch or proposal, but which you have at the ready should an investor take interest in the details and your knowledge of them
competition that may have cash prizes or mentorship opportunities for winners; you are expected to present your business idea in the form of a presentation, usually with a pitch deck
mass communication that allows companies to reach a broad audience through TV, radio, newspaper, Internet, magazines, and outdoor ads
bundle of products that is discounted to encourage customers to buy in bulk
set of unconventional practices that can help start-ups and younger firms survive in competitive markets
easiest way to price a product by taking the cost of making the product and marking it up to create profit
pricing led by what the customer is willing to pay
connecting to consumers via email or through printed mailed pieces
using lower initial prices to attract new customers and build a customer base before prices return to their standard amount
using a below-standard price to attract customers in the hope that they will purchase other, more profitable goods and services
umbrella term given to those activities that companies use to identify consumers and convert them into buyers
basic set of possible strategies and approaches that marketers use to reach their target market
psychological pricing strategy that uses odd numbers in the prices to make a product’s price point more attractive to consumers
pricing below that of a competitor’s price
company’s human resources
using face-to-face interactions to communicate and influence a customer to make a purchase
where a service takes place or a product is manufactured
channels or locations—physical or digital—where products can be found by customers
charging a price above a competitor’s price
value that must be exchanged in order for a customer to receive a product or service
chain of procedures and/or activities required to provide the product to the customer
good or a service that creates value by fulfilling a customer need or desire
amount by which revenue exceeds costs, typically described as a percent
process of communicating value to customers in a way that encourages them to buy
marketing that focuses on connecting with constituents by developing goodwill
incentives such as coupons and discounts that help attract customers and help them take action
high-pricing strategy that leverages the newness of a product in order to “skim” the most profits off the top at the beginning of a sale
online communication tool for entrepreneurs to connect with consumers, especially younger demographics
identification of cause-and effect-relationships between two or more variables
survey tool in which respondents must rank, choose, or a rate a number of “conjoint,” or linked, features or benefits
use of personal observation of the subject by being immersed in the subject’s environment

gathering of people, typically six to twelve participants, who come together to discuss a topic presented by a moderator, who usually poses questions and collects qualitative data that can be used to answer questions or define research further
analysis of the overall interest in the product or service within the industry by its target market
unmet need within a target demographic
collection and analysis of data related to a business’s target market
(or value proposition) statement of how you want the customer to perceive your company, good, or service
use of open-ended techniques such as observation, focus groups, and interviews to gain an understanding of customers’ basic reasons, opinions, and motivations
generation of numerical data through techniques such as surveys and questionnaires
group of people participating in market research
separating the total population by homogeneous groups of people that have similar tastes, backgrounds, lifestyles, demographics, and culture
portion of the market that a business can serve based on its products, services, and location
selecting a customer group based on their ability and willingness to buy
total perceived demand for a product or service within the marketplace
act of verifying that a product can be successful with a target audience
online marketing
marketing designed to move established companies and their products into new markets and territories
creative approaches to marketing that seek to gain maximum exposure through unconventional means
seeks to turn immediately available sales data into actionable and timely strategies that target the shifting landscape of consumer tastes and trends
creates customer loyalty through personal interaction and strives for long-term engagement
marketing that uses engaging content in the hopes that viewers will share it on their personal and social media networks
promotion in which a satisfied customer tells others about their positive experience with a good or service
image a company promotes and the connotations it fosters of itself and its products
someone who is a fan of your products and passes on the word to others
image a company conveys to its customers
short song or sound that brands a product or company and helps to promote it
short and catchy positioning statement that quickly communicate some core aspect of the brand to the consumer
step in the sales process in which the salesperson asks for and gets the sale
plan that the entrepreneur uses to identify and engage a consumer, from prospecting to securing the sale and beyond
wealthy, private individual seeking investment options with a greater potential return than is generally available with traditional publicly traded stocks
something of value pledged to secure a loan
borrowing funds that must be repaid, usually with interest
company lifecycle stage in which the product or service has begun development
funds provided in exchange for a share of ownership in a business
raising money to fund the startup and operation of a business
major purchases with a long life, such as buildings, land, and so on
financial resources for acquiring assets
process by which a company lists its ownership shares on a public stock exchange
company lifecycle stage in which the business has reached commercial viability
company lifecycle stage in which the business is largely still an idea
estimate of a company’s worth
individual or investment firm that specializes in funding startup companies
funds available for day-to-day operations
exchanging goods or services for other goods or services instead of for cash
nonprofit company founded for altruistic purposes
lending of funds in exchange for a promise to repay
financing with investments of small amounts of money from a large number of people
financial gift intended to support an organization’s general operations
long-term financial sustainability of an organization to fulfill its mission
financial gift intended to fund a specific goal or project
earning income from a source outside of one’s primary employ
basic offerings that a nonprofit provides to generate a portion of its revenue to sustain operations
strategy that can maintain an organization’s financial stability
treatment under U.S. tax law that removes the burden of taxes from nonprofit organizations
system of recording and classifying a company’s financial transactions and summarizing and communicating those transactions in the form of financial statements
assets = liabilities + equity
items a business owns and derives future use from
owner’s interest in the assets of the business
company’s debt to another party
account that includes vendors that the company owes money to
account that includes customers that owe the business money
financial statement that summarizes a company’s financial condition according to the accounting equation
rate at which cash outflow exceeds cash inflow
gross profit from a single item sold
cost required to produce the product or service
costs incurred in the normal course of business operations
output of an accounting system that is used to make financial decisions
section of the statement of cash flows that shows where new infusions of cash are coming from
costs that do not change, regardless of the amount of sales
selling price of a product or service minus its direct costs
financial statement that describes how much a company earned while the business was operating and what costs were incurred to generate those revenues
section of the statement of cash flows that shows major purchases of equipment or facilities
revenue minus expenses
section of the statement of cash flows that shows day-to-day activities of the business, such as purchasing supplies, paying rent, and receiving cash from customers
forecast of the future operations of a business
amount a business earns through product sales or providing a service
cost of running a business over a specified period of time
financial statement that explains the sources and uses of the company’s cash
costs that fluctuate with the level of revenue
step-by-step approach on how to maneuver a startup, how to change course if necessary, how to persevere, and how to accelerate the expansion of a business
people who like to try new things and can be found at the onset of the adoption process for a product
assesses whether the changes made to the product are creating the desired results
small changes to the current version of a product to make it better fit consumer needs
methodology entrepreneurs use this method to help them innovate by continuously testing their products and getting feedback from customers in real time
verbal delivery of an idea or business plan as a request to a group of investors by an entrepreneur
crucial change done to test a hypothesis regarding the basic product, its growth potential, and business model; if something is not working, then a change or a “pivot” needs to take place
ending a business due to the lack of goal attainment, which can include low levels of revenue and profit, or not meeting investors’ expectations
organization that researches entrepreneurship around the world
describes your venture’s unique benefits that poise it for growth
people the business will be serving, including potential customers from one or more market segments
all resources the entrepreneur will need in order to launch and sustain a business venture
actual product or service, its value proposition, and how you will reach and communicate with your target customers
strategic analytical tool used to help a potential venture or existing company identify its strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats related to business competition
marketer’s rationale as to why a product or service is different/better than the competitor’s products
brief business plan shown to potential investors and employees that is a fast and effective way to set, manage, and evaluate goals and strategies in a business
venture seeking to be a global company from its inception
stages companies go through: birth, growth (or expansion to new markets and products), maturity, decline, death or rebirth
temporary partnership in which two different enterprises combine for the purpose of mutual benefits such as sharing of expenses and to work toward shared goals and the associated potential revenue
contract in which one enterprise gives permission to another entity to manufacture and sell its products for a royalty
when two companies combine, or one buys the majority stake of the other
arrangement created by two entities to work on a project by sharing some of their strengths and resources, but not actually creating a new entity as in the case of a joint venture

story about how a successful company grew from something small into something broad and powerful, serving thousands or even millions of people; it may have marketing purposes, and it may serve as an inspirational story for leaders and employees
need to keep up with peers and the personas they represent in social settings, particularly on social media
initial section of a business plan document that summarizes the key elements of the entire plan
formal business document that used as blueprint for how a company will achieve its marketing and business goals
company’s game plan for how it will reach consumers and convert them into paying customers
examination of the internal and external circumstances relevant to a particular business
occurs when an existing business fundamentally changes their business model

developed by Osterwalder and Pigneur, used to develop a business model for a venture, including nine blocks that are mapped out to address customer segments, customer relationships, channels, revenue streams, value propositions, key partners, key activities, key resources, and cost structure

portrayal of a target customer, the most promising candidate from a business’ customer segments, which explores the understanding of that person’s condition from their perspective to understand their problems and needs

developed by Ash Maurya as a derivation of the original business model canvas; this canvas differs by addressing unfair advantages, problems, solutions, and key metrics rather than customer relationships, key partners, key activities, and key resources

modification of the business model canvas, designed for social entrepreneurship venues; new areas address impact measures around social impacts and its measurement, surplus, beneficiary segments and social and customer value propositions

one of the nine blocks on the business model canvas, this additional tool developed by Osterwalder and Pigneur is designed to illustrate a deeper dive into customer understanding and value creation of a business

feasibility analysis is the process of determining if a business idea is viable; it includes testing the viability of your product or service, evaluating your management team, assessing the market for your concept, and estimating financial viability
forecasts revenue and expenses; projects a financial narrative; and estimates project costs, valuations, and cash flow projections
determination to proceed with or abandon a plan or project
outline of preliminary expenses, operating expenses, and reserves
formal document that typically describes the business and industry, market strategies, sales potential, and competitive analysis, as well as the company’s long-term goals and objectives
shows how and why a startup has a clear if not measurable competitive advantage for relevant features/benefits by weight according to customers, such as price and quality in relation to competitors
organization formed by an alliance of business professionals who assist entrepreneurs in their startup or early stage businesses in exchange for an equity investment in the entrepreneur’s company

organization formed by a consortium of established professionals or organizations assisting startup or early stage businesses that provides office space, financial support, technical support, or advisors whose specialized skills in management and marketing complement the technical talents of the entrepreneur
formal, structured group of individuals who have a strict process or specific criteria that applicants must meet before being accepted as members

group of individuals who have a loose and flexible set of criteria for membership and function within a very broad set of guidelines; certain criteria may be required of regular members, but guests are frequently accepted with no long-term commitment expected
process of establishing and maintaining bilateral relationship with other people to exchange information, ideas, products or services, and money
informal, loosely organized group of individuals who have no formal process for joining or leaving the group, and who focus on a specific activity or social cause
group of companies or employees of companies in a specific industry, business structure, or social cause
major repairs or improvements that add to the life of an asset by improving its longevity, increasing its value or productivity, or extending its usefulness
lender’s assessment of how reliable a company is in repaying a debt
repayment of a loan, including principal and interest
organizational structure where owners, executives, or key decision makers interact directly with staff at all levels, with very few if any mid-level managers

business operations that include managing the quantity of inventory units in stock, scheduling and cataloguing replacement inventory, organizing shelf location for unit storage, paying for inventory received, and processing, fulfilling, packaging, and shipping customer orders
standard used by US federal agencies to collect, analyze, and report statistical information about businesses
financial statement that shows how much and where monies will be collected and spent in the early stages of business creation
highest volume of units that a machine can produce within a specified time period
internal comparison of actual outcomes with expected outcomes
major purchase of a functional asset that is expected to last longer than three years or that still has financial value after being fully depreciated
time when production is not occurring due to repair, restocking inventory, or unscheduled work
study of how work is performed

agreement between a bank and a depositor in which the bank makes available a maximum amount of money the depositor can borrow at any time during the term of the loan; the borrower pays a fee during the term, whether or not there is an outstanding balance, and also pays interest when there is a balance on the loan
measure of a company’s ability to cover its immediate and short-term (i.e., due within one year) debts and obligations
hiring family and close friends because of personal relationships rather than for their skill sets
number of units that can reasonably be expected to be produced within a specified time period
expenses associated with daily, ongoing activities of the business, such as inventory, office supplies, wages, insurance, and utilities
difference in unit capacity between absolute capacity and operational capacity
hiring an outside company or third party to perform a specific task, job, or process, or to manufacture goods
amount of time that a machine is actually producing products that are to be sold; also called up time
when employees do most of the work in a business
comprehensive cost of owning large capital items, including initial direct costs, operating direct costs, and indirect costs
reason the entrepreneur forms the company and determines who benefits from it, whether it is the entrepreneur, customers, or some other entity
legal organization of a business; also called entity selection
business structure designed to create profits that are distributed to the owners

business structure that is usually dedicated to furthering a particular social cause or for advocacy related to a common shared interest by using its surplus revenues to achieve its ultimate objective
business that meets a very high standard of social and environmental performance, public transparency, and accountability to balance profit with social purpose
money raised for operating a business
corporation taxed the corporate level, and then again on the owners’ personal income tax returns if corporate income is distributed to the shareholders as dividends
complex business structure created by filing the appropriate documents with the state of incorporation
company that does not allow members of the investing public to own stock; instead, it is owned by the founder’s family or friends, or a private group of investors
quasi-governmental entity, a corporation owned or sponsored by the government
entity in which members of the investing public own the stock
“pass-through” entity, also known as a small corporation, where shareholders report and claim the business’s profits as their own and pay personal income taxes on it
business created when two or more individuals or entities agree to work together to operate a business for profit

business arrangement for professional partnerships, such as law firms or accounting firms, in which partners are licensed professionals, with limited liability for financial obligations related to contracts or torts, but full liability for their own personal malpractice
business arrangement that has at least one general partner and one or more limited partners
business entity formed by two or more individuals, or partners, each of whom contributes something such as capital, equipment, or skills
integrated cross-disciplinary approach to monitoring risk
starting a new business with insufficient funds to sustain operations over an extended period of time
spreading risk over a large number of people (policyholders)
risk stemming primarily from a breach of contract, a regulatory violation, and/or the commission of a tort
flowchart diagram used to explore the outcomes of different decisions
monetary resources used by the entrepreneur in the operation of the initial startup and early business
assets that cannot be seen, touched, or felt, such as intellectual property, patents, designs, and processes
lending of funds in exchange for a promise to repay
word, phrase, symbol, or graphic that identifies the origin or source of a service
assets that have a physical form as they can be seen, touched, and felt

inflation, interest, employment, and currency exchange, rates, (among many other factors) that impact the business owner’s pricing of goods or services, the demand for such services, and the cost of production
framework that can be used to distinguish political, economic, sociocultural, and technological factors influencing access to needed resources
stem from changes in politics, such as the policies of a new presidential administration or congressional legislation, as well as of state and local offices
cost of all the resources needed to open the business to the public
specific number of units allowed into a country; sometimes quotas are limited to one source, such as one country, and sometimes the limit is placed on aggregate quantity regardless of source(s)
changes in how society is moving and the direction of that movement as it relates to a customer base and potential new markets
taxes or duties that are added to imported goods from another nation
technological equipment and tools the enterprise needs to be sure it operates efficiently

group of advisors that helps an organization make strategic decisions about the business, and brings certain knowledge and experience that can bring a new perspective on issues without having the actual authority to vote or govern
people hired by the entrepreneur to perform various duties in the business, provide customer service, or produce the goods

(also, freelancer) people or businesses that provide work similar to an employee without being part of the payroll for the contracting business, and who pay their own taxes and pay for their own benefits
creating networks with business partners through mergers, vertical integration, joint ventures, or engaging in joint political activities to slow dependency among member groups
legal agreement regarding an exit by a founding team member prior to the harvesting of the venture
formal statement that provides guidance on how ethical conduct is demonstrated
shared beliefs, values and assumptions held by people regarding how to behave or conduct themselves within the context of fitting into an organization
written document that addresses contractual topics such as division of equity, compensation, contributions to the venture, and commitments to the venture with timelines included
bar chart used to organize and plan sequential steps in a process
business that provides incomes similar to other forms of employment
formal agreement that the person signing the document will not sharing proprietary information with outside sources

tendency to continue with a predetermined action based on the past efforts and contributions to the project rather than an analysis of the current realities that disclose a new solution or approach is necessary
identified point that triggers the entrepreneur to consider what actions are needed to bring the venture back to a healthy position and whether this action is reasonable and feasible
belief that, in retrospect, the outcome was predictable when the outcome wasn’t predictable due to various uncontrollable factors
overinflated belief in oneself
creation of a plan that is too optimistic and not aligned with conservative assumptions
pattern of relying on previous actions and results as the accepted pattern and outcome with a bias against change or new information or new results

group decision-making process that includes a diverse group of people who are located at different locations and willing to respond to a series of questionnaires to assist in providing ideas, information or solutions to a defined question or problem
group decision-making process that requests multiple rounds of responses and discussions of response
goodwill that is created through helping someone else
hybrid of a corporation and a partnership that limits the owner’s liability
business entity that is owned and managed by one individual that has very little formal structure and no mandatory filing/registration with the state
raising of capital through the online sale of securities to the general public
virtual connection with a state based on sales volume or number of transactions
lean problem-solving process phase in which the entrepreneur experiments and analyzes the potential process and its capabilities
lean problem-solving process phase in which behaviors are analyzed to assess success
outside the enterprise and encompasses customers, industry trends, and competition
comprises the factors inside the enterprise, such as employees, and internal practices and processes
systematic process for the maximizing of continuous improvement and the minimization of surplus or unused material in the production of a process
lean problem-solving process phase in which the entrepreneur studies the challenge and notes all facets of the challenge requiring solution
type of graphing that permits the entrepreneur to plot each step in a process to build comprehension and detailing of the process
